body:
### Describe your issue

I have ran performance tests of cython and it seems that enabling shared utility module causes performance degradation. Here are most affected (> 10% performance degradation):

```
2026-02-11 14:12:01  ### Benchmark 'bm_slice_memoryview' (min/median/max/ ±% of median):
2026-02-11 14:12:01      Cython 'master'           = 168.789 nsec, 169.037 nsec, 169.417 nsec
2026-02-11 14:12:01      S-Cython 'master'         = 265.030 nsec, 265.359 nsec, 265.745 nsec  (   +57.0 %)
2026-02-11 14:12:01  ### Benchmark 'bm_create_inner_func_plain' (min/median/max/ ±% of median):
2026-02-11 14:12:01      Cython 'master'           = 178.644 nsec, 179.003 nsec, 179.423 nsec
2026-02-11 14:12:01      S-Cython 'master'         = 209.291 nsec, 209.592 nsec, 214.308 nsec  (   +17.1 %)
2026-02-11 14:12:01  ### Benchmark 'fused_args_1' (min/median/max/ ±% of median):
2026-02-11 14:12:01      Cython 'master'           =   2.360 usec,   2.381 usec,   2.400 usec
2026-02-11 14:12:01      S-Cython 'master'         =   3.770 usec,   3.782 usec,   3.806 usec  (   +58.9 %)
2026-02-11 14:12:01  ### Benchmark 'fused_args_2' (min/median/max/ ±% of median):
2026-02-11 14:12:01      Cython 'master'           =   1.257 usec,   1.262 usec,   1.269 usec
2026-02-11 14:12:01      S-Cython 'master'         =   1.449 usec,   1.451 usec,   1.455 usec  (   +15.0 %)
2026-02-11 14:12:01  ### Benchmark 'bm_getitem_tuple' (min/median/max/ ±% of median):
2026-02-11 14:12:01      Cython 'master'           =  36.994 usec,  37.552 usec,  38.014 usec
2026-02-11 14:12:01      S-Cython 'master'         =  42.142 usec,  42.312 usec,  42.718 usec  (   +12.7 %)
2026-02-11 14:12:01  ### Benchmark 'bm_getitem_ext' (min/median/max/ ±% of median):
2026-02-11 14:12:01      Cython 'master'           = 202.649 usec, 204.566 usec, 204.844 usec
2026-02-11 14:12:01      S-Cython 'master'         = 225.850 usec, 227.740 usec, 228.050 usec  (   +11.3 %)
2026-02-11 14:12:01  ### Benchmark 'bm_cmp_int_obj' (min/median/max/ ±% of median):
2026-02-11 14:12:01      Cython 'master'           =   1.347 usec,   1.359 usec,   1.373 usec
2026-02-11 14:12:01      S-Cython 'master'         =   1.694 usec,   1.706 usec,   1.709 usec  (   +25.5 %)
2026-02-11 14:12:01  ### Benchmark 'bm_cmp_str_obj' (min/median/max/ ±% of median):
2026-02-11 14:12:01      Cython 'master'           =   2.068 usec,   2.110 usec,   2.120 usec
2026-02-11 14:12:01      S-Cython 'master'         =   2.337 usec,   2.375 usec,   2.581 usec  (   +12.6 %)
```

Not sure if there is anything that can be done except documenting it?

comment_data.body: On Linux, you can run the benchmarks (rather, one by one) with `--perf` to create a `perf` profile and copy it into the local directory. Then you can investigate that with e.g. `perf report -i the_generated_perf_file` to find out what shared functions get called that might cause this regression. (Or pass `--callgrind` if valgrind's callgrind tool suites you better.)

> perf report -i the_generated_perf_file to find out what shared functions get called that might cause this regression
I ran the bm_cython with `--perf` and it is interesting:

The puzzling part is why shared utility has so high `__Pyx_Raise`: 
```
+    4.16%     0.68%  python   _cyutility.cpython-314-x86_64-linux-gnu.so  [.] __Pyx_Raise                                                ▒
```

If I had to guess: `__Pyx_Raise` looks like it's got a lot of branches. But it also looks like many of them can probably be eliminated by the C compiler in the usage in `RaiseStatNode`. So it's probably inlined and simplified in real code.

The `raise` path is probably usually a slow and unusual path anyway. Hopefully nobody's calling `raise` in a loop and hoping it'll be fast. But it's probably a good example of why things might be slower
The fact it doesn't appear at all in the non-shared version probably says that it's inlined rather than saying that it's not taking time

>> The puzzling part is why shared utility has so high `__Pyx_Raise`:
>
> it's probably inlined and simplified in real code.

Right, I think it just makes the difference between 10.03% in the shared case and 12.17% in the non-shared case for `__pyx_pw_9bm_cython_7_with_contextmanager_raise`. I don't think exception raising is what we should chase first, but I'd also guess that inlining of _something_, possibly more than one thing, adds up to the perceived performance difference.

The `bm_cython` module only has micro benchmarks for single language features. Might not be the best to start with.

Also, the results are skewed because the "context manager with exception raising" part takes so much time overall, so the other benchmarks are pushed down because they simply occupy much less wall clock time.

What can definitely be seen is that memory views are slower. The `unpack_buffer` test take ~50% more time in the shared case, mostly due to its children (i.e. functions that it calls). Could you run the matrix benchmark? That might be more conclusive here.

One more comment on `__Pyx_Raise`:

I suspect the most used cases are `__Pyx_Raise(type, NULL, NULL, NULL)` and `__Pyx_Raise(type, value, NULL, NULL)`. If we felt it was important enough we could put a 1 arg and 2 arg interface (which then just calls the full `__Pyx_Raise`) so you get the common cases inlined.

I'm not hugely sure it's worth it for this case (and it would also slightly increase the size of the shared module) but it's an approach that might help.

In the case of builtin exceptions, we even know at compile time when a class is raised or an instance, and could call an appropriate function just for that. Especially the `raise instance` case is really trivial and wouldn't need shared code at all. I've created https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/7523 for this, also to keep this ticket from derailing. Exception raising is not a performance problem.

> Could you run the matrix benchmark?

Sure. Here are results:

```
python Demos/benchmarks/run_benchmarks.py  -b bm_matrix --show-size --with-shared-module master
...
```

(This comment is largely duplicated from one on scikit-image)

Looking at the scikit-image issue that prompted this: I think they're specifically suffering because (in a particular microbenchmark...) 

```
flags[i:i_ahead] = CANDIDATE
```

is generating a call to `__pyx_memoryview_slice_memviewslice`.

Looking at the Cython source for `__pyx_memoryview_slice_memviewslice` I think it's another case where there's a lot of easily eliminated branches that get spotted when it's part of the same file. At very least the parameters int have_start, int have_stop, int have_step, bint is_slice all look like they eliminate large chunks of it in the C compiler. And probably also the handling of negative step.

There's basically three choices from a Cython point of view:

* We live with it - the decision to use the shared library is a trade-off.
* We split the function into a variety of smaller functions capturing the different combinations and expose those in the shared library.
* We take this specific function out of the shared-library on the basis that it's almost always inlined into something trivial so most likely isn't a useful space-saving.

I think option 3 is the right one.

> I think option 3 is the right one.

I agree. The second option might also be viable in some cases, but three seems best here.

> Looking at the scikit-image issue that prompted this

Presumably https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/8050

> It seems that exported is `__pyx_memoryview_check` which should be `inline` function

It's duplicated in the .pxd file as an inline function, though:

https://github.com/cython/cython/blob/5992db289706b5062b1fc78b2ff94eb0787a8566/Cython/Utility/MemoryView.pxd#L76-L78

And it seems that the test modules in the shared module test pick it up correctly and use the inlined function.
Nothing wrong here, IIUC.

> And it seems that the test modules in the shared module test pick it up correctly and use the inlined function.
Nothing wrong here, IIUC.

I messed up with an example. My point was that **all** functions from `MemoryView.pyx` are exported in `__Pyx_modinit_Function_export_code()`. We should export **only** functions that are present in `MemoryView.pxd`. I am not C expert but I suppose that C Compiler does not inline function when the code contains pointer to it: 

```c
void (*const __pyx_export_pointers[])(void) = {(void (*)(void))&_unellipsify, ...
```

This can limit the C compiler in inlining internal _cyutility functions. Or am I wrong?
BTW. other that my point, I agree with @da-woods  and @scoder.

> I am not C expert but I suppose that C Compiler does not inline function when the code contains pointer to it:

It's forced to create a non-inlined version of it. That costs a little bit of space. It can still choose to inline it in other places.

It may well be worth restricting what it exports to cut the size down further but I don't think it should cause performance issues.

> We take this specific function out of the shared-library on the basis that it's almost always inlined into something trivial so most likely isn't a useful space-saving.

Will inline-ing the function too? I declared it as inline and doublechecked the generated file, the function was replicated in both of them - see: https://github.com/matusvalo/cython/commit/1bbe7ea3f0e9a6b7188e6525bf1346c458767307

The performance results are:

